title: Lineage of The Siege of Jerusalem
summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of the 1993 University of Michigan CME XML representation of Hoyt N. Duggan's electronic transcription. Duggan worked against Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day's 1932 EETS OS 188 edition and reported intermittent manuscript emendation, especially in lines 200-600. Kölbing and Day used Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 656 (L) as copy-text and collated the six other witnesses then known: A, C, D, E, U, and V. Princeton P and the Exeter binding fragment Ex were discovered after that edition, so its subtitle all other extant MSS must not be read as all nine witnesses known today. The current OTA deposit preserves three text files, including Duggan's complete conventional line sequence 1-1334. By contrast, the U-M and repository XML contains 1,234 L elements labeled 1-997 and 1098-1334: the 100-line gap is a property of this electronic representation, not of the abstract work. The Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive now provides separate descriptions, diplomatic transcriptions, and view-only images for all nine surviving witnesses. A dated institution-by-institution audit found no complete public host-codex facsimile for A, C, D, E, L, P, U, or V; the whole surviving one-strip Ex carrier is viewable through SJEA. Huntington's official partial scan of E includes all target folios 205r-216r, while the Bodleian publishes only a two-image 35mm-slide selection for L. No complete public scan of the 1932 edition was found. Rights and provider terms are stated only for the exact electronic, edition, transcription, and image layers to which the evidence applies.

agents.name: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
Hoyt N. Duggan
notes: Prepared the electronic transcription represented by OTA 1357 and the U-M CME item.

agents.name: Mabel Day
notes: Completed and revised Kölbing's unfinished edition and supplied its introduction, notes, and glossary.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for SJerus
entities.description: The checked-in partial XML representation used by this repository.
notes: The artifact has 1,234 L elements, covering printed line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334; its l.* IDs instead run sequentially from l.1 through l.1234.
The thirteen DIV1 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups rather than authorial sections.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item SJerus
entities.description: A 1993 structured electronic representation of Duggan's transcription, incomplete at conventional lines 998-1097.
notes: The header calls the text unproofread and reports intermittent manuscript emendation, especially in lines 200-600.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive deposit 1357
alternate_labels: The siege of Jerusalem / compiled by H. Duggan
entities.description: The current OTA deposit containing three text bitstreams: lines 1-200, lines 601-997 plus 1098-1334, and Duggan's complete working transcription of conventional lines 1-1334.

entities.label: Kölbing-Day edition of The Siege of Jerusalem (1932)
entities.description: The immediate printed source of Duggan's electronic transcription; based on L and collated against the six other witnesses then known.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day, eds., The Siege of Jerusalem: Edited from MS. Laud Misc. 656 with Variants from All Other Extant MSS., Early English Text Society, Original Series 188 (London: H. Milford, Oxford University Press, 1932), xxxi, 133 pp., frontispiece.
bibliographic.title: The Siege of Jerusalem: Edited from MS. Laud Misc. 656 with Variants from All Other Extant MSS.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 188
bibliographic.publisher: H. Milford, Oxford University Press, for the Early English Text Society

notes: The subtitle all other extant MSS referred to the seven witnesses known by 1932. P and Ex were not known to the editors.
Kölbing's text and variants for approximately lines 1-580 had begun appearing in 1898; Day revised and completed the edition.

entities.label: Kraus reprint of Kölbing-Day (1971)
entities.description: A 1971 reprint of EETS OS 188 available through controlled digital lending; it is not a newly collated edition.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing and Mabel Day, eds., The Siege of Jerusalem, EETS OS 188 (New York: Kraus Reprint Co., 1971), reprint of the 1932 edition.
bibliographic.title: The Siege of Jerusalem
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 188

entities.label: Michael Livingston's METS edition (2004)
entities.description: A later reading edition using L as base text and emending it chiefly from P, A, and D; it is a contextual modern edition, not an ancestor of the 1993 U-M encoding.

bibliographic.citation: Michael Livingston, ed., The Siege of Jerusalem (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2004), Middle English Texts Series.
bibliographic.title: The Siege of Jerusalem

entities.label: Stinson, Makeres of the Mind (2011)
entities.description: A peer-reviewed study of editorial practice and digital editions that documents the nine-witness tradition, Kölbing-Day's use of L as copy-text, that edition's relatively light emendation, and its lack of access to P and Ex.

bibliographic.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, 'Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem,' Yearbook of Langland Studies 25 (2011): 39-62, doi:10.1484/J.YLS.1.102108.
bibliographic.title: Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem

notes: This is supporting scholarship about the edition and witness history, not an edition or textual ancestor of the repository artifact.

entities.label: Swanton, A Further Manuscript of The Siege of Jerusalem (1990)
entities.description: The two-page scholarly article that first identifies and physically describes the Exeter binding fragment Ex, Devon Record Office Deposit 2507, and relates its two surviving textual portions to the Kölbing-Day text.

bibliographic.citation: Michael Swanton, 'A Further Manuscript of The Siege of Jerusalem,' Scriptorium 44, no. 1 (1990): 103-104, doi:10.3406/scrip.1990.1568.
bibliographic.title: A Further Manuscript of The Siege of Jerusalem

notes: This is supporting description of Ex, not an edition or textual ancestor of the witness.

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 31042 (A)
entities.description: Robert Thornton's paper anthology; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 50r-66r.

notes: A lost leaf after fol. 53 causes the absence of conventional lines 293-369.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A II, part I (C)
entities.description: A paper miscellany; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 111r-125r.

date_statements.label: Target-copy date
date_statements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century, probably circa 1446-1461

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Caligula A II, part I
holding.notes: Target work at fols. 111r-125r within the ff. 3-139 component.

notes: A lost leaf after fol. 112 causes the absence of conventional lines 167-248.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 491, part I (D)
entities.description: A mixed paper-and-parchment manuscript; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 206r-227v.

date_statements.label: Target-copy date
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century

notes: A generally complete witness copied by a professional London scribe whose forms have been associated with Rayleigh, Essex.

entities.label: The Huntington Library, HM 128 (E)
entities.description: A parchment manuscript; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 205r-216r.

date_statements.label: Target-copy date
date_statements.value: Early fifteenth century; specialist analysis proposes approximately 1435 for the target hand

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 205r-216r; current Huntington Digital Library object https://hdl.huntington.org/digital/collection/p15150coll7/id/50420/.

notes: A generally complete witness; the target text is assigned to the manuscript's fifth hand.

entities.label: Devon Archives, Deposit 2507 binding fragment (Ex)
entities.description: A single paper binding strip preserving two discontiguous portions of The Siege of Jerusalem.

date_statements.label: Target-copy date
date_statements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century

holding.notes: Binding fragment discovered during restoration; work portions correspond approximately to Hanna-Lawton lines 985-1017 and 1106-1138.

notes: The two textual portions are non-contiguous and must not be described as a complete copy.

entities.label: Bodleian Libraries, MS Laud Misc. 656 (L)
entities.description: The oldest generally complete witness and the copy-text of the 1932 and 2004 editions; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 1v-19r.

date_statements.label: Current institutional codex date
date_statements.notes: The official catalog dates the codex more broadly/later than specialist Siege descriptions; both assertions are retained.

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 1v-19r; exact work catalog record https://medieval.bodleian.ox.ac.uk/catalog/work_14646.

notes: Specialist analysis associates the target scribe's language with northwestern Oxfordshire; this is witness provenance, not authorial dialect.

entities.label: Princeton University Library, Taylor Medieval MS. 11 (P)
entities.description: A late-fourteenth-century parchment witness, incomplete at the end; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 104v-110v.

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 104v-110v; current catalog identifier 99111942213506421.

notes: The copy ends imperfectly around Hanna-Lawton line 1143 and is largely illegible after approximately line 1055.
The witness was unknown to Kölbing and Day and came to scholarly notice after a 1952 sale.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Mm.V.14 (U)
entities.description: A parchment manuscript copied by Richard Frampton; The Siege of Jerusalem is at fols. 187r-206v.

date_statements.label: Target-copy date
date_statements.value: Beginning of the fifteenth century; its identified scribe is documented as active from at least 1402 to 1416

notes: A generally complete witness copied by the professional London scribe Richard Frampton.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI (V)
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment component; the surviving Siege text at fols. 70r-75v begins imperfectly.

holding.notes: Target work at fols. 70r-75v within the ff. 38-85 component.

notes: The surviving text begins around Hanna-Lawton line 966; two lost preceding quires probably contained the opening.

entities.label: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive transcriptions and descriptions
entities.description: A scholarly electronic archive with separate XML diplomatic transcriptions and descriptions for all nine surviving witnesses.

entities.label: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive witness images
entities.description: View-only page images for the surviving Siege leaves of all nine witnesses; these are work-leaf selections, not claims of complete digital facsimiles of every host codex.
entities.reproduction_format: Web page images linked to manuscript transcriptions

entities.label: Bodleian two-image selection for MS Laud Misc. 656
entities.description: A two-canvas 35mm-slide selection showing the inside upper board and fol. 1v; only fol. 1v overlaps The Siege of Jerusalem.
entities.reproduction_format: Two digitized 35mm slides delivered by IIIF

entities.label: Huntington Digital Library partial scan of HM 128
entities.description: An official 144-canvas partial scan of the 219-folio host codex. It includes the complete target-work sequence at fols. 205r-216r but is not a complete facsimile of HM 128.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with web-delivered page images

subject.description: All checked-in XML content: conventional line labels 1-997 and 1098-1334

object.description: The same partial U-M electronic representation

scope.notes: The repository preserves the partial U-M representation rather than reconstructing its missing 100 lines.

object.description: Duggan transcription lines represented in both systems

scope.notes: The shared authorship, legacy OTA identifier, and line-level correspondence establish versions of the same electronic transcription; the exact production direction is not documented.
Duggan reports periodic manuscript emendation against the Day-Kölbing edition, so this is not described as a mechanically exact transcription.
L is the edition's base/copy-text; light emendation and variant collation mean the edition is not a diplomatic transcript.
Siglum A is resolved by the edition tradition and current archive.

subject.description: The article's editorial-history discussion, especially pp. 41-43

object.description: The 1932 Kölbing-Day edition and its relation to the witness tradition

scope.notes: Stinson identifies L as copy-text, describes the edition as lightly emended, and explains that P and Ex became known only after it appeared.

object.description: The complete surviving one-strip Exeter binding fragment Ex

scope.notes: Swanton identifies the strip, describes its physical and textual extent, and compares its readings with the Kölbing-Day text.
A reissue, not a revised or newly collated edition.
Livingston explicitly identifies L as base text but emends it substantially and regularizes presentation.

subject.description: Emended reading text and textual notes
Emended reading text and textual notes

object.description: Alternative gamma-tradition text where P is lacking

subject.description: Emended reading text and textual notes

No complete open scan of the exact 1932 edition was found; use a holding library or the controlled 1971 reprint.
Borrow through Internet Archive controlled digital lending; the complete PDF is not an unrestricted public download.

notes: The current NCSU URL returns bytes identical to the cached PDF. pdfinfo reports 24 pages and pdftotext extracts pp. 39-62; qpdf succeeds with recoverable shared-object and linearization hint-table warnings, and the bytes were not rewritten.

access.access_method: Choose a witness and diplomatic view in the scholarly archive; exact transcription and description XML files are also publicly retrievable.
View images inside the archive's witness interface; provider terms prohibit downloading from the site and require permission for uses beyond scholarly/private study.
notes: No SJEA manuscript images were downloaded into the source cache because the site's terms expressly prohibit downloading images.

access.access_method: The current Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record reports no digitised content. Use British Library Digitisation Services to ask about photography of the complete host or target fols. 50r-66r.
The current Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record reports no digitised content. Use British Library Digitisation Services to ask about photography of the complete host or target fols. 111r-125r.

No complete public scan of the 329-folio host was located in the current Lambeth digital collections. SJEA displays the target folios only; Lambeth Imaging Services accepts priced orders using shelfmark MS 491 and exact folio references, subject to preservation review and a 40-image limit per order.

local_copies.label: Lambeth Digital Photography order form and terms

access.access_method: View the official 144-canvas partial scan in Huntington Digital Library or through its IIIF manifest. The sequence covers the complete target work at fols. 205r-216r but only selected portions of the 219-folio host.

local_copies.label: Complete 144-canvas IIIF bundle for the official partial facsimile

work_portion.label: Complete target-work sequence inside the partial host scan
notes: Canvas 106 is the f. 204v-205r opening containing the target incipit; canvas 141 is f. 216r containing the target explicit.

The ZIP contains the complete IIIF manifest, a per-image inventory, and all 144 published JPEGs requested at 3000 pixels wide. Every source image is at least 4645 pixels wide, so no image was upscaled. ZIP integrity, 144 per-image SHA-256 values, and image decoding were verified.

access.access_method: The official object explicitly says HM 128 was only partially scanned. Contact Huntington Library Rights and Permissions about unscanned folios or a complete-host reproduction; physical consultation is restricted and requires curatorial approval.
View both sides of the complete surviving one-strip physical carrier in SJEA. The images are view-only under SJEA terms and were not downloaded.
notes: Complete refers to the single surviving binding strip, not a complete medieval copy of the poem or its former host volume.

access.access_method: Submit a Devon Archives Research and Copying request quoting Deposit 2507 and ask for a digital image; publication or online reuse requires separate written permission.
access.format: JPEG by default; other digital formats may be requested

access.access_method: No currently working complete public surrogate was verified. Princeton's older two-part full-scan PDF endpoints now return 404 after redirect, and the current catalog record is protected by a traffic-control challenge. Submit the exact catalog URL and shelfmark through Ask Special Collections; its current service accepts digitization orders up to 300 pages and lists USD 2 per image for non-Princeton researchers.

No complete public CUDL or ArchiveSearch surrogate for Mm.V.14 was located. Cambridge notes that many medieval manuscripts remain discoverable only through printed catalogs; request priced target or complete-host images through the Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory.
The current Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record reports no digitised content. Use British Library Digitisation Services to ask about photography of the complete host or target fols. 70r-75v.
Consult the current manuscript and exact work records.
access.format: HTML and TEI XML catalog records

access.access_method: Open the two-canvas Digital Bodleian object or use its IIIF Presentation manifest.

local_copies.label: Complete two-canvas IIIF bundle at 1800-pixel width

work_portion.label: Opening target-work leaf
notes: Canvas 1 is the inside upper board and contains no target text.

The ZIP passed unzip -t and contains manifest.json, inventory.json, and exactly two JPEG images. Complete coverage refers to the published two-canvas facsimile entity, not to MS Laud Misc. 656 or even all Siege leaves.

access.access_method: The current catalog exposes only two selected 35mm-slide images; ask Bodleian's priced Imaging Services about a complete codex surrogate or targeted reproduction of fols. 1v-19r.

rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph
rights.basis: The poem was created anonymously in the late fourteenth century, many centuries beyond the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 section 12 duration applicable to literary works and anonymous authorship. Modern editorial, encoding, and image layers remain separate.

rights.component: Underlying anonymous late-fourteenth-century poem text only, excluding every modern transcription, edition, markup, interface, and photograph
rights.basis: The anonymous poem's original expression dates to the late fourteenth century and is outside any surviving federal copyright term. The U.S. Copyright Office explains that protection is time-limited and that expiration places a work in the public domain; separate modern editorial and digital contributions are not included in this conclusion.

rights.component: U-M electronic transcription, character conversion, structural markup, metadata, and web presentation, excluding the underlying medieval poem
rights.jurisdiction: United States; provider statement not located
rights.basis: The 1993 XML publication statement says the item was available through U-M and commercially from OTA but gives no reuse license. The current U-M item was verified only as an access endpoint; no item-specific current rights statement was retrieved. OTA's present CC BY-NC-SA assertion is not transferred to U-M's separate markup and presentation.

rights.component: The exact copied repository XML bytes and inherited electronic markup, excluding the underlying medieval poem text
rights.jurisdiction: Repository and source-provider terms; United States
rights.basis: No repository-level or file-specific license for the copied SJerus XML was found. The artifact is a pinned copy of U-M-origin markup; neither the medieval poem's public-domain status nor OTA's current deposit license is silently applied to these exact repository bytes.

rights.component: The three text bitstreams as distributed by the current OTA record
rights.basis: The current OTA item record asserts a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 license for the deposited resource.
notes: This provider assertion is not transferred automatically to the medieval work, the 1932 printed edition, U-M markup, or manuscript images.

rights.component: 1932 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, apparatus, and book design
rights.basis: No source-specific open license or definitive rights statement for the complete 1932 edition was found; editorial and typographical layers must be assessed separately from the medieval text.
notes: Do not infer public-domain status for every jurisdiction merely from the medieval content or publication date.

rights.component: Internet Archive controlled-lending surrogate of the 1971 reprint
rights.basis: The Internet Archive item is access-restricted to controlled digital lending rather than offered as an unrestricted public-domain download.

rights.component: METS reading edition PDF and web presentation
rights.basis: METS states that its digital editions are copyrighted and intended for individual educational use; its separate CC BY-NC-SA license for downloadable XML does not automatically license the PDF or manuscript images.

rights.component: SJEA descriptions, diplomatic transcriptions, markup, and site presentation
rights.basis: The archive publishes the XML openly but no source-specific open license for the transcription and markup files was found on the checked pages.
notes: The explicit no-download and permission language on the terms page concerns images; it is not silently transferred to the transcription XML.

rights.component: Manuscript photographs displayed by the Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive
rights.basis: SJEA states that image rights remain with the owning libraries, permits free viewing for scholarly and private study, prohibits downloading images from the site, and requires written permission for other use.

rights.component: The two published Bodleian digital images and their derivatives
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and asserts Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 for the published images.
notes: The license is attached to the published image layer, not the medieval text or physical manuscript.

rights.component: The exact 144 manuscript photographs and IIIF derivatives published in Huntington Digital Library for HM 128
rights.basis: Huntington's current Using the Library page says Huntington Digital Library high-resolution images may be accessed and downloaded free. Its Library Rights and Permissions page does not assert an image license: it says no Huntington permission is required for public-domain material or copyright the Library does not own, asks users to cite specific items, and leaves copyright-holder identification, permission, and fair-use assessment to the researcher. Those statements support access and caching but do not establish that the modern photographs are public domain or openly licensed.
notes: Neither the medieval manuscript text's public-domain status nor SJEA's or Bodleian's separate image terms are transferred to this Huntington image layer.

rights.component: Stinson's article text and the complete NCSU-hosted PDF
rights.jurisdiction: United States and applicable copyright jurisdictions
rights.basis: The complete 2011 article is freely downloadable from the current NCSU host, but neither the PDF nor its host supplies a source-specific open reuse license. Public access is not treated as permission to reuse the article or publisher-formatted PDF.

rights.component: Swanton's article text and Persée rendered page images
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright jurisdictions
rights.basis: Persée marks the article free to access and provides rendered pages, but the article record supplies no source-specific open reuse license for the text or page images. Free access is not treated as a public-domain or open-license assertion.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The full printed edition and a complete 1-1334 Duggan working transcription
editorial_practices.representation: Poem lines 1-997 and 1098-1334 only; front matter, back matter, editorial notes, printed illustrations, and conventional lines 998-1097 absent
editorial_practices.description: The U-M electronic representation omits the printed edition's paratexts and illustrations and contains a 100-line textual gap not present in the complete OTA working transcription.

editorial_practices.representation: Intermittent manuscript emendation, especially in lines 200-600
editorial_practices.description: Duggan states that he emended from the manuscripts against the Day-Kölbing edition from time to time, especially in a separately prepared lines 200-600 section; the individual manuscripts are not identified.

editorial_practices.source_feature: L as copy-text and the six other manuscripts known by 1932
editorial_practices.representation: A lightly emended edited text of L with variant readings from A, C, D, E, U, and V
editorial_practices.description: Kölbing and Day based the text on L and collated all other witnesses then known; P and Ex were not available to them.

editorial_practices.source_feature: L as base text, with primary emendation from P, A, and D
editorial_practices.representation: A substantially emended reading edition with selected alternatives rather than a full nine-witness apparatus
editorial_practices.description: Livingston uses P and then A for the gamma branch and D for the delta branch when emending L; readers are directed to Hanna and Lawton for a full apparatus of all extant witnesses.

editorial_practices.representation: Editorial capitalization; thorn rendered th and yogh rendered g or y according to orthography and alliteration
editorial_practices.description: The edition regularizes capitalization and transliterates thorn and yogh rather than reproducing manuscript letter forms diplomatically.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript punctuation, word division, and larger textual divisions
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial punctuation and hyphenation, quatrain layout, and a prologue plus six passus
editorial_practices.description: Punctuation and capitalization are editorial; compounds may be hyphenated, and the poem is laid out in reconstructed quatrains and passus rather than simply reproducing L's three major divisions.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Nine host manuscripts and one binding fragment
editorial_practices.representation: Images of leaves carrying surviving Siege text
editorial_practices.description: The archive's image corpus is scoped to the work's surviving leaves and fragment rather than asserted as nine complete host-codex facsimiles.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_OTA/SJerus.xml.
evidence.summary: The source file identifies the 1993 U-M encoding, Duggan's unproofread manuscript emendation, OTA legacy identifier, and Kölbing-Day 1932 source edition; it states that printed front/back matter, notes, and illustrations are omitted.

evidence.citation: Structural and line-label inspection of the checked-in SJerus XML, 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: The XML has 1,234 L, 13 DIV1, 71 PB, and 14 HEAD elements. Printed N labels run 1-997 and 1098-1334, while L IDs run l.1-l.1234; conventional lines 998-1097 are absent.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The siege of Jerusalem, item SJerus.
evidence.summary: The stable item identifiers resolve to U-M's CME representation. Automated access received a provider access-control response during checking, but the XML's own publication statement documents the public CME item.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive / University of Oxford, The siege of Jerusalem, handle 20.500.14106/1357.
evidence.summary: The record names Hoyt Duggan, gives current repository dates and a CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 assertion, and exposes three public text files.

evidence.citation: Line-label and content comparison of OTA 1357 bitstreams with the checked-in SJerus XML, 2026-07-15.
evidence.locator: All three OTA bitstreams and XML L elements
evidence.summary: The complete working file contains conventional lines 1-1334. The other files contain 1-200 and 601-997 plus 1098-1334. Corresponding line text agrees with the U-M representation after expected character conversion and markup, while only the U-M/XML representation lacks 998-1097.
notes: This comparison establishes related versions and the location of the gap, not an undocumented direction of copying between OTA and U-M systems.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, The Siege of Jerusalem, EETS OS 188, record 34776.
evidence.summary: The record supplies the 1932 edition's title, editors, imprint, series, extent, and note that Day completed and revised Kölbing's unfinished edition.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive, siegeofjerusalem0000ekol, 1971 Kraus reprint of The Siege of Jerusalem.
evidence.summary: The item identifies the 1971 Kraus reprint and restricts full-book access to controlled digital lending; only metadata was cached.

evidence.citation: Michael Livingston, ed., The Siege of Jerusalem, Middle English Texts Series, 2004.
evidence.locator: Introduction pp. 8-13 and 36-40; manuscript list; complete edited text
evidence.summary: The edition describes all nine witnesses and the stemma, uses L as base text, identifies P/A/D as primary emendation sources, and states its regularization and division practices.
notes: The 143-page PDF opens and extracts successfully. qpdf succeeds with recoverable object-offset warnings; no repair was applied.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright and Citation.
evidence.locator: Digital-edition use and XML license statements
evidence.summary: METS distinguishes copyrighted digital editions intended for individual educational use from downloadable XML files licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, 'Makeres of the Mind: Authorial Intention, Editorial Practice, and The Siege of Jerusalem,' Yearbook of Langland Studies 25 (2011): 39-62, doi:10.1484/J.YLS.1.102108.
evidence.locator: pp. 41-43, witness list and comparison of the Kölbing-Day and Hanna-Lawton editions
evidence.summary: Stinson lists all nine witnesses, explains that P and Ex became known only after the Kölbing-Day edition, and identifies L as that edition's copy-text while characterizing its emendation as light relative to Hanna-Lawton.
notes: The current NCSU URL returns 566,433 bytes identical to the cached PDF. The 24-page file opens and extracts successfully; qpdf reports recoverable shared-object and linearization hint-table warnings, and no repair was applied.
Neither the PDF nor the current NCSU download host supplies a source-specific open reuse license.

evidence.citation: Timothy L. Stinson, ed., Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive.
evidence.summary: The archive identifies all nine surviving witnesses and provides their descriptions, diplomatic transcriptions, and work-leaf images.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, Terms and Conditions for Use of Images.
evidence.summary: The terms retain image rights with owning libraries, permit free viewing for scholarly/private study, prohibit downloading images from the site, and require written permission for other use.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript A description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, material, provenance, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies BL Add MS 31042, fols. 50r-66r, Robert Thornton's mid-fifteenth-century paper copy, and its lost-leaf gap.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript C description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, material, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies Cotton Caligula A II part I, fols. 111r-125r, as a later-fifteenth-century paper copy and documents its lost-leaf gap.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript D description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, material, scribe, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies Lambeth MS 491 part I, fols. 206r-227v, as an early-fifteenth-century mixed-support copy associated with a professional London scribe.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript E description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, material, hands, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies Huntington HM 128, fols. 205r-216r, as a parchment copy and associates the target text with an early-to-mid-fifteenth-century hand.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript Ex description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, dimensions, and fragment scope
evidence.summary: Identifies Devon Deposit 2507 as one paper binding strip preserving two discontiguous fifteenth-century Siege portions.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript L description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, support, dimensions, dialect, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies Bodleian Laud Misc. 656, fols. 1v-19r, as a late-fourteenth-century parchment copy and provides target-specific physical and scribal description.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript P description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, target folios, dialect, and incomplete ending
evidence.summary: Identifies Princeton Taylor Medieval MS. 11, fols. 104v-110v, as a late-fourteenth-century parchment witness with an incomplete and increasingly illegible ending.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript U description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, support, scribe, and target folios
evidence.summary: Identifies CUL Mm.V.14, fols. 187r-206v, as an early-fifteenth-century parchment copy by Richard Frampton.

evidence.citation: Siege of Jerusalem Electronic Archive, manuscript V description XML.
evidence.locator: Identification, date, support, target folios, and missing opening
evidence.summary: Identifies Cotton Vespasian E XVI, fols. 70r-75v, as a mid-fifteenth-century parchment fragment beginning around line 966 after lost quires.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 31042.
evidence.summary: The current interim record identifies Thornton's collection, the Siege rubric and colophon, target folio 50 onward, paper support, and fifteenth-century date.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A II.
evidence.summary: The current interim record identifies the paper host and its second-half-fifteenth-century Middle English ff. 3-139 component containing the target folios.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI.
evidence.summary: The current interim record identifies the Cotton manuscript and indicates no digitized content; specialist description supplies the target folios and fragment scope.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation services.
evidence.locator: Images from our collection; manuscript-order guidance
evidence.summary: The Library accepts priced image orders using exact shelfmarks and folio references; current item records for A, C, and V report no public digitised content.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library, Imaging Services and Digital Photography order form.
evidence.locator: How to Order Images; Digital Photography form
evidence.summary: Lambeth accepts image orders keyed by shelfmark and folio, subject to preservation review and a forty-image limit per order. A dated search of its current whole-volume and image collections found no complete public MS 491 host facsimile; SJEA supplies the target leaves.

evidence.citation: Huntington Digital Library, HM 128, Prick of conscience; Piers Plowman; and other works, object p15150coll7/50420 and IIIF manifest.
evidence.summary: The official record describes HM 128 as a 219-folio host only partially scanned for Digital Scriptorium. Its 144-canvas IIIF sequence nevertheless includes the complete target work from the opening on fol. 205r through the explicit on fol. 216r.
notes: The complete published-object bundle contains the manifest, inventory, and all 144 non-upscaled 3000-pixel-wide JPEGs; ZIP and per-image integrity checks passed.

evidence.citation: The Huntington, Using the Library.
evidence.summary: The current official page states that high-resolution Huntington Digital Library images can be accessed and downloaded free.
notes: Cached as rendered HTML through a headless browser because the command-line HTTP endpoint returned a Vercel 429 checkpoint.

evidence.citation: The Huntington, Library Rights and Permissions.
evidence.locator: Quoting or Reproducing Huntington Library Materials; Crediting and Citing The Huntington
evidence.summary: The current page does not claim a blanket image license: it waives Huntington permission for public-domain or Huntington-nonowned rights, requests item citation, and assigns copyright-holder identification, permissions, and fair-use assessment to the researcher.
notes: Cached as rendered HTML through a headless browser because the command-line HTTP endpoint returned a Vercel 429 checkpoint.

evidence.citation: Devon Archives and Local Studies, Research and Copying.
evidence.summary: The current service accepts exact-reference orders for digital images and requires separate written permission before supplied copies are published or uploaded.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library, Taylor Medieval MS. 11 catalog record and Special Collections Digitization Services.
evidence.summary: The current Special Collections service accepts digitization requests up to 300 pages. The catalog endpoint currently presents a traffic-control challenge, and the two older official Taylor MS. 11 part-PDF URLs redirect to missing files, so no working complete public surrogate was verified.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Archives, Modern and Medieval Manuscripts search guidance and Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory.
evidence.locator: Medieval manuscript search guidance; Request copies or images
evidence.summary: CUL notes that many medieval manuscripts remain described only in printed catalogs and provides an exact image-request portal. Dated CUDL and ArchiveSearch searches located no complete public Mm.V.14 host surrogate.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Order images from Imaging Services.
evidence.summary: Bodleian accepts priced image orders for collection material not available through Digital Bodleian or Bodleian Images, using exact shelfmarks and folio references.

evidence.citation: Michael Swanton, A Further Manuscript of The Siege of Jerusalem, Scriptorium 44.1 (1990), p. 103.
evidence.summary: Introduces the binding fragment discovered during restoration and identifies its physical context.
notes: The exact rendered-page URL returns a PNG despite its .jpg suffix. Persée record metadata marks access free but supplies no source-specific open reuse license.

evidence.citation: Michael Swanton, A Further Manuscript of The Siege of Jerusalem, Scriptorium 44.1 (1990), p. 104.
evidence.summary: Describes the two discontiguous passages preserved by the fragment and its significance as an additional witness.
notes: The exact rendered-page URL returns a PNG despite its .jpg suffix. Persée record metadata marks access free but supplies no source-specific open reuse license.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Laud Misc. 656 and The siege of Jerusalem, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries.
evidence.summary: The official catalog identifies the parchment codex, the target work, beginning-of-fifteenth-century codex date, and availability of only two selected images.
notes: Cached from the Bodleian catalog source repository at commit e865840e73308c58846a24da10c431a88193ddd2.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, MS. Laud Misc. 656, two-image 35mm-slide selection, IIIF manifest 83c8088e-4188-4a7e-ae80-f59d64bab428.
evidence.locator: Canvases 1-2; target-work overlap at canvas 2, fol. 1v
evidence.summary: The manifest publishes exactly two images, inside upper board and fol. 1v, attributes them to Bodleian Libraries, and asserts CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: The complete two-canvas bundle passed ZIP integrity testing and contains manifest.json, inventory.json, and exactly two JPEG derivatives.

evidence.citation: United Kingdom, Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, section 12, Duration of copyright in literary, dramatic, musical or artistic works.
evidence.summary: The official consolidated statute supplies the United Kingdom duration rules used to assess the underlying anonymous medieval literary work separately from modern editions and reproductions.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, What Is Copyright?, duration and public-domain overview.
evidence.locator: How long does copyright protection last? and public-domain explanation
evidence.summary: The official Copyright Office overview explains that copyright protection is time-limited and that works enter the public domain when the applicable term expires; it links to the detailed duration circular.

open_questions.question: What exact production step caused conventional lines 998-1097 to disappear from the U-M/XML representation although they survive in Duggan's complete OTA working transcription?
next_steps: Seek U-M HTI or OTA processing documentation for item U*-1357-A.
Compare any surviving pre-SGML transfer files and conversion logs rather than inferring direction from directory names.

open_questions.question: Which specific manuscript witnesses did Duggan consult for his intermittent emendations, especially in conventional lines 200-600?
next_steps: Locate Duggan's transcription documentation, correspondence, or deposited working notes.
Do not create direct U-M-to-witness collation relations until the consulted witnesses can be identified.

open_questions.question: Is a complete, lawful, openly downloadable scan of the exact 1932 Kölbing-Day edition available?
next_steps: Check EETS/OUP and research-library digitization services for the 1932 printing rather than treating the controlled 1971 reprint as open.
If a scan is found, verify title page, edition date, completeness, rights, and byte integrity before caching it.

open_questions.question: Can a complete digital facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 656, or at least all target folios 1v-19r, be obtained with clear reuse terms?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Imaging Services whether a complete surrogate already exists and request pricing and reuse terms for the entire codex or fols. 1v-19r.
Keep the two-canvas Digital Bodleian selection explicitly partial relative to the manuscript.

open_questions.question: How should the current Bodleian beginning-of-fifteenth-century codex date be reconciled with specialist late-fourteenth-century dating of the target Siege copy?
next_steps: Consult the full codicological arguments behind each date and determine whether they address the same codex component and target hand.
Preserve both attributed statements until their scopes and evidence can be reconciled.

review_notes: No direct copied_from relations are asserted among the nine extant witnesses. Modern stemmatic grouping supports textual families but does not identify a surviving manuscript as the physical exemplar of another.
Kölbing-Day's six comparison-witness relations are limited to A, C, D, E, U, and V. P and Ex are later discoveries and are intentionally absent from that edition's upstream chain.

SJEA image terms were not transferred to transcription XML; the Bodleian image license and Huntington provider statements remain separate image layers and were not transferred to the medieval text, physical manuscripts, or one another.
All ignored cache files used by access or evidence records are hard-linked to the canonical main-checkout cache and are not part of the proposed Git commit.
